Figure 4. Whole-brain task activation for lexical processing. Brain activation maps depict voxels significantly 9 engaged during word recognition in the whole-brain level (voxel-level threshold uncorrected p < .001, cluster extent 10 threshold k ≥ 30). Red = Inflected > Baseline, Blue = Simple > baseline, and Magenta = overlap of the two 11 contrasts. 12 13

Figure 2. Theoretical frequency-based representational dissimilarity matrices (RDMs). Each matrix encodes the 3 pairwise lexical distance that served as a model predictor in the RSA. Word-frequency RDMs (Zipf-word; panels A–4 C) and stem-frequency RDMs (Zipf-Stem; panels D–F) were computed as the absolute difference (i.e., Euclidean 5 distance in a 1-D frequency space) in Zipf-scale frequency between two items, yielding symmetrical matrices whose 6 values increase with greater frequency disparity. For both frequency measures we constructed three matrices: all 7 nouns, only morphologically inflected nouns, and only simple nouns. Warmer colors denote larger frequency gaps; 8 cooler colors denote smaller gaps. 9
Table 2. MNI coordinates of significant activation identified in the whole-brain analysis. 1

Figure 6. Regions of interest univariate analysis results. (A) Locations of anatomically defined ROIs: IFG pars 2 opercularis (IFGoper), IFG pars triangularis (IFGtri), supramarginal gyrus (SMG), angular gyrus (AG), middle 3 temporal gyrus (MTG), and fusiform gyrus (FuG). (B) Predicted BOLD β as a function of Zipf word frequency for 4 inflected (blue) and simple (yellow) nouns, estimated from linear mixed-effects models. The Condition × Frequency 5 interaction was significant in SMG (p = .042) and marginal in AG (p = .07). (C) The same analysis for Zipf stem 6 frequency. Shaded ribbons represent 95% confidence intervals. *p < .05; †p < .10. 7 8 9
Table 1. Descriptive statistics of the stimuli set. 1

Figure 1. Frequency distributions across morphological conditions. (A) All words (N = 120) show overlapping distributions for 9 surface frequency (blue) and base frequency (red). (B) Inflected nouns (N = 60) show distinct distributions for surface and base 10 frequency measures. Histograms use 15 bins; shaded regions indicate overlapping frequencies. (C) Simple nouns (N = 60) 11 display only surface frequency, as surface equals base by definition for monomorphemic words. 12 13 Procedure 14 A rapid event-related fMRI design was employed using a visual lexical decision task. 15 Participants were instructed to respond, as quickly and accurately as possible, whether each 16 presented stimulus is a real word or not. Each trial began with a fixation cross (+) displayed for 17 100 ms, followed by a target stimulus presented at the center of the screen for 1000 ms. After 18 pressing response button, blank screen was shown during the inter-trial interval (ITI), which was 19 jittered with an average duration of 4000 ms (range: 1000–7000 ms). Figure 3. Mixed-effect model predictions for behavioral performance. Predicted reaction times (left y-axes), log-14 transformed milliseconds and predicted probability of correct response (right y-axes) are shown as functions of (A) 15 word frequency (Zipf-word) and (B) stem frequency (Zipf-stem). Blue lines represent inflected nouns; yellow lines 16 represent simple nouns; shaded ribbons indicate 95% confidence intervals (CIs). 17
